      Elon vs. OpenAI
   =20
 =20

I=C2=A0wrote yesterday=C2=A0about reports that the US Securities and Exchan=
ge Commission might be looking into whether OpenAI=C2=A0or its founder and =
chief executive officer, Sam Altman, might have misled its investors. Late =
last year, OpenAI=E2=80=99s board briefly   fired Altman for not being =E2=
=80=9Cconsistently candid,=E2=80=9D and then reversed course and fired itse=
lf instead. So=C2=A0there is some reason to believe that=C2=A0somebody wasn=
=E2=80=99t candid about something.
I had my doubts that it would rise to the level of securities fraud, though=
. For one thing, OpenAI is a  nonprofit organization, and even its for-prof=
it subsidiary, OpenAI Global LLC, which=C2=A0has=C2=A0raised money from=C2=
=A0investors, isn=E2=80=99t all that=C2=A0for-profit. I wrote:=C2=A0
At the top of OpenAI=E2=80=99s operating agreement, it warns investors: =E2=
=80=9CIt would be wise to view any investment in OpenAI Global, LLC in the =
spirit of a donation, with the understanding that it may be difficult to kn=
ow what role money will play in a post-[artificial general intelligence] wo=
rld.=E2=80=9D I still don=E2=80=99t know what Altman was supposedly not can=
did about, but whatever it was, how material can it possibly have been to i=
nvestors, given what they signed up for? =E2=80=9COoh he said it cost $50 m=
illion to train this model but it was really $53 million=E2=80=9D or whatev=
er, come on, the investors were donating money, they=E2=80=99re not sweatin=
g the details.
But that wasn=E2=80=99t quite right, was it?=C2=A0Nonprofits can defraud th=
eir donors. Generally that sort of fraud is not about=C2=A0financial result=
s; it is about the nonprofit=E2=80=99s mission, and whether it is using the=
 donors=E2=80=99 money to advance that mission.=C2=A0If I ask you to donate=
 to save the whales, and you give me $100,000 earmarked to save the whales,=
 and I spend it all on luxury vacations for myself, I probably will get in =
trouble.=C2=A0I suppose if Altman was not candid about OpenAI=E2=80=99s mis=
sion, or its pursuit of that mission, that really could have been a kind of=
=C2=A0fraud on OpenAI=E2=80=99s donors. I mean investors. It could have bee=
n donation/securities fraud on the donors/investors.
Here=E2=80=99s  one of them!

Elon Musk sued OpenAI and its Chief Executive Officer Sam Altman, alleging =
they violated the artificial intelligence startup=E2=80=99s founding missio=
n by putting profit ahead of benefiting humanity.
The 52-year-old billionaire, who was a co-founder of OpenAI but is no longe=
r involved, said in a lawsuit filed late Thursday in San Francisco that the=
 company=E2=80=99s close relationship with Microsoft Corp. has undermined i=
ts original mission of creating open-source technology that wouldn=E2=80=99=
t be subject to corporate priorities.
Musk, who is also CEO of Tesla Inc., has been among the most outspoken abou=
t the dangers of AI and artificial general intelligence, or AGI. The releas=
e of OpenAI=E2=80=99s ChatGPT more than a year ago popularized advances in =
AI technology and raised concerns about the risks surrounding the race to d=
evelop AGI, where computers are as smart as an average human.
=E2=80=9CTo this day, OpenAI Inc.=E2=80=99s website continues to profess th=
at its charter is to ensure that AGI =E2=80=98benefits all of humanity,=E2=
=80=99=E2=80=9D the lawsuit said. =E2=80=9CIn reality, however, OpenAI Inc.=
 has been transformed into a closed-source de facto subsidiary of the large=
st technology company in the world: Microsoft.=E2=80=9D

Here is  Musk=E2=80=99s complaint. It is essentially a complaint for breach=
 of contract: Musk argues that he founded OpenAI with Altman and Greg Brock=
man, that they had a deal about how OpenAI would operate, and that Altman a=
nd Brockman have now gone back on the deal. The contract said that OpenAI w=
ould be a nonprofit, that it would be run for the benefit of humanity, that=
 it would build artificial general intelligence=C2=A0and give it away for f=
ree, and that it would build open-source software (thus the name) and expla=
in to=C2=A0the public how its models operate. But now=C2=A0OpenAI is run fo=
r profit, for the benefit of Microsoft and its other investors rather than =
humanity. It has built artificial general intelligence and=C2=A0is hoarding=
 it for its own enrichment rather than giving it away.
One problem with this claim is that the contract doesn=E2=80=99t quite=C2=
=A0exist. Musk=E2=80=99s lawsuit says that OpenAI has breached =E2=80=9Cthe=
 Founding Agreement=E2=80=9D of OpenAI, capitalized like that, as though he=
, Altman and Brockman sat down and signed a piece of paper with =E2=80=9CFo=
unding Agreement=E2=80=9D at the top,=C2=A0setting out how OpenAI would ope=
rate. But they didn=E2=80=99t. From the complaint:
This Founding Agreement is memorialized in, among other places, OpenAI, Inc=
.=E2=80=99s founding Articles of Incorporation and in numerous written comm=
unications between Plaintiff and Defendants over a multi-year period. =E2=
=80=A6
That is: There is no document titled =E2=80=9CFounding Agreement=E2=80=9D; =
despite being wealthy sophisticated=C2=A0repeat startup founders who know a=
 lot of lawyers, the founders never sat down and signed a contract. Instead=
, the =E2=80=9CFounding Agreement=E2=80=9D has to be=C2=A0inferred from oth=
er documents. Musk cites two:
=09There=E2=80=99s a June 2015 email from Altman to Musk, with five numbere=
d bullet points setting out a plan for building AI. =E2=80=9CThe mission wo=
uld be to create the first general AI and use it for individual empowerment=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0i.e., the distributed version of the future that seems the =
safest,=E2=80=9D the first bullet point begins. =E2=80=9CI think ideally we=
=E2=80=99d start with a group of 7-10 people, and plan to expand it from th=
ere,=E2=80=9D says the second, and =E2=80=9Cwe have a nice extra building i=
n Mountain View they can have.=E2=80=9D The third bullet point proposes a f=
ive-person governance board including Musk and Altman, and =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=
=80=99d have an ongoing conversation about what work should be open-sourced=
 and what shouldn=E2=80=99t.=E2=80=9D In the fourth bullet point, Altman as=
ks Musk to =E2=80=9Cbe involved somehow in addition to just governance=E2=
=80=9D; maybe he could =E2=80=9Ccome by and talk to them about progress onc=
e a month or whatever.=E2=80=9D
  [1]
 Musk replied to the email =E2=80=9CAgree on all.=E2=80=9D=09There is the D=
ecember 2015 certificate of incorporation of OpenAI Inc., the nonprofit cor=
poration that ultimately controls OpenAI. =E2=80=9CThe specific=C2=A0purpos=
e of this corporation is to provide funding for research, development and d=
istribution of technology related to artificial intelligence,=E2=80=9D it s=
ays. =E2=80=9CThe resulting technology will benefit the public and the corp=
oration will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when app=
licable. The corporation is not organized for the private gain of any perso=
n. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0No part of the net income or assets of this corporation s=
hall ever inure to the benefit of any director, officer or member thereof o=
r to the benefit of any private person.=E2=80=9D

And Musk donated money to OpenAI, the nonprofit, over the years: In 2016 an=
d 2017, he was the biggest donor to OpenAI, and =E2=80=9Call told, Mr. Musk=
 contributed more than $44 million to OpenAI, Inc. between 2016 and Septemb=
er 2020.=E2=80=9D He also did other stuff for OpenAI: He helped=C2=A0with r=
ecruitment, paid rent on its offices, =E2=80=9Cregularly visited,=E2=80=9D =
and =E2=80=9Cwas present for important company milestones.=E2=80=9D He ulti=
mately  left his role with OpenAI in 2018.
You can sort of wave your hands at all this and say =E2=80=9CMusk had a con=
tract with OpenAI in which he agreed to donate money and in exchange OpenAI=
 explicitly agreed to be an open-source nonprofit forever,=E2=80=9D but I d=
on=E2=80=99t think that=E2=80=99s exactly right? The email from Altman was =
an initial proposal, not a detailed contract setting out the permanent term=
s of their deal; it promised not to open-source the software forever but on=
ly to =E2=80=9Chave an ongoing conversation about what work should be open-=
sourced and what shouldn=E2=80=99t.=E2=80=9D Money was not mentioned.
And the certificate of incorporation was not a contract between Musk and Op=
enAI: He didn=E2=80=99t sign the certificate, and he wasn=E2=80=99t a share=
holder, because there were no shares (it=E2=80=99s a nonprofit). OpenAI=E2=
=80=99s fiduciary duties are not to him, as a co-founder, but to humanity. =
The evidence of a=C2=A0specific deal=C2=A0between Musk and OpenAI is pretty=
 thin.
Still, I sympathize? OpenAI Inc., the top-level company that controls OpenA=
I=E2=80=99s business, really is incorporated as a nonprofit. It really was =
formed to work for the benefit of humanity and not =E2=80=9Cfor the private=
 gain of any person.=E2=80=9D And it really did take donations from Musk an=
d use them to build its team. But it eventually set up a=C2=A0for-profit su=
bsidiary, OpenAI Global LLC, which has managed to raise money from investor=
s at an $86 billion valuation, and those investors (and OpenAI=E2=80=99s em=
ployees =E2=80=94=C2=A0though not Altman) expect=C2=A0some (capped) financi=
al return on that investment.  OpenAI says that=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cit became inc=
reasingly clear that donations alone would not scale with the cost of compu=
tational power and talent required to push core research forward, jeopardiz=
ing our mission=E2=80=9D: It had to raise money from investors, by promisin=
g them returns, to achieve its mission. (It raised something like $130.5 mi=
llion in total donations; it has raised something like   $13 billion in inv=
estment commitments from Microsoft.)
I am sure OpenAI had good lawyers when it set up this structure, and I assu=
me=C2=A0that as a technical matter none of this violates the certificate of=
 incorporation or the nonprofit mission: A portion of the profits of=C2=A0O=
penAI Global LLC can go to employees and venture capitalists and Microsoft,=
 even though =E2=80=9Cno part of the net income or assets of=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=
OpenAI Inc.=C2=A0can. Still that is rather technical, and Musk has a point =
here:
In 2017, Mr. Brockman and others suggested transforming OpenAI, Inc. from a=
 nonprofit to a for-profit corporation. After a series of communications ov=
er several weeks, Mr. Musk told Mr. Brockman, Dr. Sutskever, and Mr. Altman=
 =E2=80=9C[e]ither go do something on your own or continue with OpenAI as a=
 nonprofit. I will no longer fund OpenAI until you have made a firm commitm=
ent to stay or I=E2=80=99m just being a fool who is essentially providing f=
ree funding to a startup. Discussions are over.=E2=80=9D
That was before OpenAI launched its for-profit subsidiary, though. When it =
did, Musk sort of grudgingly tolerated it
  [2]
:

On March 11, 2019, OpenAI, Inc. announced that it would be creating a for-p=
rofit subsidiary: OpenAI, L.P.
  [3]
 Prospective investors were notified of an =E2=80=9Cimportant warning=E2=80=
=9D at the top of the summary term sheet that the for-profit entity =E2=80=
=9Cexists to advance OpenAI Inc.=E2=80=99s [the nonprofit=E2=80=99s] missio=
n of ensuring that safe artificial general intelligence is developed and be=
nefits all of humanity. The General Partner=E2=80=99s duty to this mission =
and the principles advanced in the OpenAI Inc. Charter take precedence over=
 any obligation to generate a profit.=E2=80=9D Accordingly, investors were =
expressly advised that =E2=80=9C[i]t would be wise to view any investment i=
n OpenAI LP in the spirit of a donation.=E2=80=9D
Following the announcement, Mr. Musk reached out to Mr. Altman asking him t=
o =E2=80=9Cbe explicit that I have no financial interest in the for-profit =
arm of OpenAI.=E2=80=9D However, Mr. Musk continued to support OpenAI, Inc.=
, the non-profit, donating an additional $3.48 million in 2019.

But at that point he was=C2=A0just providing free funding to a startup, was=
n=E2=80=99t he?
So Musk argues that they had a deal, and that OpenAI breached it in three w=
ays. First, it licenses GPT-4, its most powerful model so far, to Microsoft=
. OpenAI, in its public statements and its charter and its deal with Micros=
oft, has said that it will seek to=C2=A0build=C2=A0artificial general intel=
ligence=C2=A0for the benefit of humanity, but that it can license lesser fo=
rms of artificial intelligence to Microsoft. So the question is: Is GPT-4 a=
rtificial general intelligence? Musk says yes:

GPT-4 is not just capable of reasoning. It is better at reasoning than aver=
age humans. It scored in the 90th percentile on the Uniform Bar Exam for la=
wyers. It scored in the 99th percentile on the GRE Verbal Assessment. It ev=
en scored a 77% on the Advanced Sommelier examination. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0
GPT-4 is an AGI algorithm, and hence expressly outside the scope of Microso=
ft=E2=80=99s September 2020 exclusive license with OpenAI. =E2=80=A6
Under its new Board, it is not just developing but is actually refining an =
AGI to maximize profits for Microsoft, rather than for the benefit of human=
ity.=C2=A0

Seems like a stretch, though Musk quotes =E2=80=9CMicrosoft=E2=80=99s own r=
esearchers=E2=80=9D saying that GPT-4 =E2=80=9Ccould reasonably be viewed a=
s an early (yet still incomplete version of an artificial general intellige=
nce (AGI) system.=E2=80=9D
Second, GPT-4 is not open-source:
GPT-4=E2=80=99s internal design was kept and remains a complete secret exce=
pt to OpenAI=E2=80=94and, on information and belief, Microsoft. There are n=
o scientific publications describing the design of GPT-4. Instead, there ar=
e just press releases bragging about performance. On information and belief=
, this secrecy is primarily driven by commercial considerations, not safety=
. Although developed by OpenAI using contributions from=C2=A0Plaintiff and =
others that were intended to benefit the public, GPT-4 is now a de facto Mi=
crosoft proprietary algorithm, which it has integrated into its Office soft=
ware suite.
Again, there doesn=E2=80=99t seem to be=C2=A0any actual agreement between O=
penAI and Musk (or anyone else) promising to make everything open-source, b=
ut, sure, it=E2=80=99s annoying that they built a model partly using his mo=
ney and now won=E2=80=99t let him see it.
Third, Musk objects to OpenAI =E2=80=9Cpermitting Microsoft, a publicly tra=
ded for-profit corporation, to occupy a seat on OpenAI, Inc.=E2=80=99s Boar=
d of Directors and exert undue influence and control over OpenAI=E2=80=99s =
non-profit activities.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Microsoft is   just an observer on the=
 board, not a voting member, but, right, its interests in OpenAI are probab=
ly=C2=A0not particularly charitable.
And Musk is asking the court, not for his donations back, but for an order =
making OpenAI do what it supposedly promised to do: Open up GPT-4=E2=80=99s=
 source code, make it freely available to the public, end Microsoft=E2=80=
=99s exclusive license and board rights, and generally stop OpenAI=E2=80=99=
s for-profit work.
Obviously one should be pretty cynical=C2=A0here.=C2=A0Musk runs a for-prof=
it artificial intelligence company, xAI, which competes with OpenAI and has=
   raised money by   citing OpenAI=E2=80=99s commercial success. Blowing up=
 that competitor=E2=80=99s commercial prospects, as this lawsuit is trying =
to do, could help xAI. He also runs=C2=A0other companies =E2=80=94=C2=A0Tes=
la Inc., Twitter/X =E2=80=94=C2=A0that make use of AI. I suppose Musk=E2=80=
=99s companies would benefit from reading OpenAI=E2=80=99s source code and =
=E2=80=9Cscientific publications describing the design of GPT-4,=E2=80=9D s=
o why not sue OpenAI and try to make that information public?=C2=A0Musk=E2=
=80=99s protests about OpenAI=E2=80=99s unseemly pursuit of AI profit for i=
nvestors do look a little insincere, since he=E2=80=99s doing the same thin=
g.
But he does have kind of a reasonable gripe? OpenAI was founded as a nonpro=
fit, raised a bunch of money from him as a donor to a nonprofit, and is now=
 somehow an enormously=C2=A0valuable tech startup owned by people who are n=
ot him.=C2=A0After OpenAI fired Altman last November, but before it brought=
 him back, it looked as if OpenAI=E2=80=99s=C2=A0for-profit investors=C2=A0=
=E2=80=94=C2=A0Microsoft, but also some venture capitalists, and the employ=
ees who owned quasi-equity =E2=80=94=C2=A0had lost a bunch of value. I  wro=
te at the time:=C2=A0
I feel like the lesson here is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t invest=
 in nonprofits at an $86 billion valuation.=E2=80=9D Which I think has neve=
r come up before? Like as far as I can tell no one in human history has eve=
r purchased shares in a nonprofit at an $86 billion valuation? Because purc=
hasing shares in a nonprofit, at any valuation, is not a coherent thing to =
do? But then OpenAI made it happen, for the first time, and probably also t=
he last.
But it turns out the investors were fine,
  [4]
=C2=A0and the correct lesson might be the opposite one: Don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0=
donate money to a nonprofit that is selling shares at an $86 billion valuat=
ion!
  [5]
 You might be skeptical about putting an $86 billion valuation on a nonprof=
it, but probably you should be even more skeptical that a business with an =
$86 billion valuation is a nonprofit.

      HBS Ponzi
   =20
 =20

Oh man, I kind of wish I had  thought of this:

An alleged Ponzi scheme run by a Harvard MBA who solicited money from fello=
w alumni of the prestigious US business school has been shut down by a New =
York court after collecting more than $2.9mn.
New York attorney-general Letitia James said Thursday her office had secure=
d the court order to freeze funds controlled by Vladimir Artamonov, after b=
eing alerted of the suicide of one of his clients who lost $100,000.

=E2=80=9CThat guy was my Harvard Business School classmate=E2=80=9D is such=
 a powerful vetting mechanism: You get to rely on the gatekeeping function =
of HBS (=E2=80=9Cthat guy is probably smart=E2=80=9D), the reputational cap=
ital it confers (=E2=80=9Cthat guy probably isn=E2=80=99t running a Ponzi b=
ecause that would be a waste of the value of his Harvard MBA=E2=80=9D) and =
also the weak ties of the alumni network (=E2=80=9Cthat guy sat in front of=
 me in Marketing, he seemed fine=E2=80=9D).=C2=A0From James=E2=80=99 press =
release:

Artamonov identified many of his investors through the HBS alumni network.=
=C2=A0Many of his investors did not have a close personal relationship with=
 him and only knew him as an acquaintance. =E2=80=A6
Since 2021, Artamonov has secured at least $2.9 million from at least 29 in=
dividual investors and engaged in a Ponzi scheme by paying the existing inv=
estors with the new investors=E2=80=99 funds. ...=C2=A0Artamonov also used =
his investors=E2=80=99 money to fund unauthorized personal expenses for vac=
ations, shopping, and dining.=C2=A0

Also his pitch was wild:
The OAG alleged=C2=A0that Artamonov lured clients by claiming that he could=
 learn which investments Berkshire Hathaway would make ahead of the market =
by examining public state insurance filings. Artamonov boasted to his inves=
tors that it is like =E2=80=9Chaving a private time machine=E2=80=9D and =
=E2=80=9Cgetting tomorrow=E2=80=99s newspaper today,=E2=80=9D and projected=
 returns of 500-1,000 percent. In reality, Artamonov used his investors=E2=
=80=99 money to buy short-term options that expired within days of purchase=
 and appeared to have no relation to Berkshire Hathaway or its investment a=
ctivities.=C2=A0Artamonov lost millions of investors=E2=80=99 funds by inve=
sting in these short-term options, but did not disclose the loss to his inv=
estors. To cover up the losses, Artamonov=C2=A0told the investors that it h=
ad been a =E2=80=9Cquiet=E2=80=9D month and to just wait and see.
=E2=80=9CBerkshire Hathaway is required to publicly disclose its investment=
s in state insurance filings before it discloses them in SEC filings, and I=
 will read the insurance filings to make informed trades=E2=80=9D is =E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0wrong, sure, but it has the vague shape of something that could be=
 true. But what about =E2=80=9C=E2=80=A6=C2=A0and those informed trades wil=
l generate projected returns of 1,000%=E2=80=9D? What are they teaching kid=
s at Harvard these days? I=C2=A0feel like if=C2=A0I=C2=A0were pitching a Po=
nzi scheme to the Harvard Business School alumni, I would be promoting fake=
 returns of, like, 35% with a high Sharpe ratio. You know: good, but not=C2=
=A0obviously fake. But apparently =E2=80=9CI have a time machine that gives=
 me guaranteed 1,000% returns=E2=80=9D works on Harvard MBAs too.

rnment on AI safety and regulation, which the complaint says was eventually=
 published in October 2015 =E2=80=9Cand signed by over eleven thousand indi=
viduals, including Mr. Musk, Stephen Hawking and Steve Wozniak.=E2=80=9D


  [2] =E2=80=9CElon Musk claims he was offered shares in OpenAI at =E2=80=
=98various points=E2=80=99 but says it =E2=80=98seemed unethical/illegal to=
 accept them,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Business Insider  noted last month, and =E2=
=80=9CMusk added it wasn't clear to him how the company's structure was leg=
al.=E2=80=9D


  [3] This is now OpenAI Global LLC, more or less. See page 17 of the compl=
aint on =E2=80=9COpenAI=E2=80=99s Shifting Corporate Structure.=E2=80=9D


  [4] Until this lawsuit.


  [5] This is subtly different from the case of donating to Harvard. Also, =
to be clear, Musk stopped donating in about 2020, before OpenAI=E2=80=99s v=
aluation got *that* high.

5px;">Elon vs. OpenAI</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlua=
W9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDItMjkvdGhlLWJvYXJkLW9mLWRpcmVjdG9ycy1pcy1pbi1jaGFy=
Z2U_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMwMTI0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmN=
lPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjQwMzAxJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f0=
7ceef4b8524a319a23cB51a786de" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope=
" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;">wrote yesterday</a>=C2=A0about report=
s that the US Securities and Exchange Commission might be looking into whet=
her OpenAI=C2=A0or its founder and chief executive officer, Sam Altman, mig=
ht have misled its investors. Late last year, OpenAI=E2=80=99s board briefl=
y <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34535030.256376/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTEtMjAvd2h=
vLWNvbnRyb2xzLW9wZW5haT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzAxMjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPW=
VtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDAzMDEmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduP=
W1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB98faba0d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" it=
emscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decor=
ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  fired Altman</a=
> for not being =E2=80=9Cconsistently candid,=E2=80=9D and then reversed co=
urse and fired itself instead. So=C2=A0there is some reason to believe that=
=C2=A0somebody wasn=E2=80=99t candid about something.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I had my doubts that it would rise to the leve=
l of securities fraud, though. For one thing, OpenAI is a <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly9vcGVuYWk=
uY29tL291ci1zdHJ1Y3R1cmU/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4177dd42" itemprop=3D"Sto=
ryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; =
text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-c=
olor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> nonpro=
fit organization</a>, and even its for-profit subsidiary, OpenAI Global LLC=
, which=C2=A0<em>has=C2=A0</em>raised money from=C2=A0investors, isn=E2=80=
=99t all <em>that=C2=A0</em>for-profit. I wrote:=C2=A0</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At the top of OpenAI=E2=80=99s ope=
rating agreement, it warns investors: =E2=80=9CIt would be wise to view any=
 investment in OpenAI Global, LLC in the spirit of a donation, with the und=
erstanding that it may be difficult to know what role money will play in a =
post-[artificial general intelligence] world.=E2=80=9D I still don=E2=80=99=
t know what Altman was supposedly not candid about, but whatever it was, ho=
w material can it possibly have been to investors, given what they signed u=
p for? =E2=80=9COoh he said it cost $50 million to train this model but it =
was really $53 million=E2=80=9D or whatever, come on, the investors were do=
nating money, they=E2=80=99re not sweating the details.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But that wasn=E2=80=99t quite right, was it?=
=C2=A0Nonprofits <em>can</em> defraud their donors. Generally that sort of =
fraud is not about=C2=A0<em>financial results</em>; it is about the nonprof=
it=E2=80=99s mission, and whether it is using the donors=E2=80=99 money to =
advance that mission.=C2=A0If I ask you to donate to save the whales, and y=
ou give me $100,000 earmarked to save the whales, and I spend it all on lux=
ury vacations for myself, I probably will get in trouble.=C2=A0I suppose if=
 Altman was not candid about OpenAI=E2=80=99s mission, or its pursuit of th=
at mission, that really could have been a kind of=C2=A0fraud on OpenAI=E2=
=80=99s donors. I mean investors. It could have been donation/securities fr=
aud on the donors/investors.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bl=
oombergbusiness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvb=
S9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDMtMDEvbXVzay1zdWVzLW9wZW5haS1hbHRtYW4tZm9yLWJy=
ZWFjaGluZy1maXJtLXMtZm91bmRpbmctbWlzc2lvbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzAxMjRfTU9ORVlTVFV=
GRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDAzMD=
EmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB066d0140" itempr=
op=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;=
"> one of them</a>!</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elon Musk sued OpenAI and its Chief Executive =
Officer Sam Altman, alleging they violated the artificial intelligence star=
tup=E2=80=99s founding mission by putting profit ahead of benefiting humani=
ty.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The 52-year-old billionaire, who was a co-foun=
der of OpenAI but is no longer involved, said in a lawsuit filed late Thurs=
day in San Francisco that the company=E2=80=99s close relationship with Mic=
rosoft Corp. has undermined its original mission of creating open-source te=
chnology that wouldn=E2=80=99t be subject to corporate priorities.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Musk, who is also CEO of Tesla Inc., has been =
among the most outspoken about the dangers of AI and artificial general int=
elligence, or AGI. The release of OpenAI=E2=80=99s ChatGPT more than a year=
 ago popularized advances in AI technology and raised concerns about the ri=
sks surrounding the race to develop AGI, where computers are as smart as an=
 average human.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CTo this day, OpenAI Inc.=E2=80=99s we=
bsite continues to profess that its charter is to ensure that AGI =E2=80=98=
benefits all of humanity,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D the lawsuit said. =E2=80=9CIn r=
eality, however, OpenAI Inc. has been transformed into a closed-source de f=
acto subsidiary of the largest technology company in the world: Microsoft.=
=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVu=
dHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JZQ1Vtd0E0WHhwdy92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
B61e1da22" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;"> Musk=E2=80=99s complaint</a>. It is essentially a compl=
aint for breach of contract: Musk argues that he founded OpenAI with Altman=
 and Greg Brockman, that they had a deal about how OpenAI would operate, an=
d that Altman and Brockman have now gone back on the deal. The contract sai=
d that OpenAI would be a nonprofit, that it would be run for the benefit of=
 humanity, that it would build artificial general intelligence=C2=A0and giv=
e it away for free, and that it would build open-source software (thus the =
name) and explain to=C2=A0the public how its models operate. But now=C2=A0O=
penAI is run for profit, for the benefit of Microsoft and its other investo=
rs rather than humanity. It has built artificial general intelligence and=
=C2=A0is hoarding it for its own enrichment rather than giving it away.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One problem with this claim is that the contra=
ct doesn=E2=80=99t <em>quite=C2=A0</em>exist. Musk=E2=80=99s lawsuit says t=
hat OpenAI has breached =E2=80=9Cthe Founding Agreement=E2=80=9D of OpenAI,=
 capitalized like that, as though he, Altman and Brockman sat down and sign=
ed a piece of paper with =E2=80=9CFounding Agreement=E2=80=9D at the top,=
=C2=A0setting out how OpenAI would operate. But they didn=E2=80=99t. From t=
he complaint:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This Founding Agreement is memoria=
lized in, among other places, OpenAI, Inc.=E2=80=99s founding Articles of I=
ncorporation and in numerous written communications between Plaintiff and D=
efendants over a multi-year period. =E2=80=A6</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is: There is no document titled =E2=80=9C=
Founding Agreement=E2=80=9D; despite being wealthy sophisticated=C2=A0repea=
t startup founders who know a lot of lawyers, the founders never sat down a=
nd signed a contract. Instead, the =E2=80=9CFounding Agreement=E2=80=9D has=
 to be=C2=A0inferred from other documents. Musk cites two:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">There=E2=80=99s a June 2015 email fro=
m Altman to Musk, with five numbered bullet points setting out a plan for b=
uilding AI. =E2=80=9CThe mission would be to create the first general AI an=
d use it for individual empowerment =E2=80=94=C2=A0i.e., the distributed ve=
rsion of the future that seems the safest,=E2=80=9D the first bullet point =
begins. =E2=80=9CI think ideally we=E2=80=99d start with a group of 7-10 pe=
ople, and plan to expand it from there,=E2=80=9D says the second, and =E2=
=80=9Cwe have a nice extra building in Mountain View they can have.=E2=80=
=9D The third bullet point proposes a five-person governance board includin=
g Musk and Altman, and =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99d have an ongoing conversation a=
bout what work should be open-sourced and what shouldn=E2=80=99t.=E2=80=9D =
In the fourth bullet point, Altman asks Musk to =E2=80=9Cbe involved someho=
w in addition to just governance=E2=80=9D; maybe he could =E2=80=9Ccome by =
and talk to them about progress once a month or whatever.=E2=80=9D<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a> Musk replied to the email =E2=80=9CAgree on all.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li s=
tyle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">There is the December 2015 certificate of inco=
rporation of OpenAI Inc., the nonprofit corporation that ultimately control=
s OpenAI. =E2=80=9CThe specific=C2=A0purpose of this corporation is to prov=
ide funding for research, development and distribution of technology relate=
d to artificial intelligence,=E2=80=9D it says. =E2=80=9CThe resulting tech=
nology will benefit the public and the corporation will seek to open source=
 technology for the public benefit when applicable. The corporation is not =
organized for the private gain of any person. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0No part of the=
 net income or assets of this corporation shall ever inure to the benefit o=
f any director, officer or member thereof or to the benefit of any private =
person.=E2=80=9D</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And Musk donated money to OpenAI, the nonprofi=
t, over the years: In 2016 and 2017, he was the biggest donor to OpenAI, an=
d =E2=80=9Call told, Mr. Musk contributed more than $44 million to OpenAI, =
Inc. between 2016 and September 2020.=E2=80=9D He also did other stuff for =
OpenAI: He helped=C2=A0with recruitment, paid rent on its offices, =E2=80=
=9Cregularly visited,=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cwas present for important compa=
ny milestones.=E2=80=9D He ultimately <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VtYWZvci5jb20vYXJ0aWN=
sZS8wMy8yNC8yMDIzL3RoZS1zZWNyZXQtaGlzdG9yeS1vZi1lbG9uLW11c2stc2FtLWFsdG1hbi=
1hbmQtb3BlbmFp/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1d2a268d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" it=
emscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decor=
ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> left his role wi=
th OpenAI in 2018</a>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You can sort of wave your hands at all this an=
d say =E2=80=9CMusk had a contract with OpenAI in which he agreed to donate=
 money and in exchange OpenAI explicitly agreed to be an open-source nonpro=
fit forever,=E2=80=9D but I don=E2=80=99t think that=E2=80=99s exactly righ=
t? The email from Altman was an initial proposal, not a detailed contract s=
etting out the permanent terms of their deal; it promised not to open-sourc=
e the software forever but only to =E2=80=9Chave an ongoing conversation ab=
out what work should be open-sourced and what shouldn=E2=80=99t.=E2=80=9D M=
oney was not mentioned.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And the certificate of incorporation was not a=
 contract between Musk and OpenAI: He didn=E2=80=99t sign the certificate, =
and he wasn=E2=80=99t a shareholder, because there were no shares (it=E2=80=
=99s a nonprofit). OpenAI=E2=80=99s fiduciary duties are not to him, as a c=
o-founder, but to humanity. The evidence of a=C2=A0<em>specific deal=C2=A0<=
/em>between Musk and OpenAI is pretty thin.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still, I sympathize? OpenAI Inc., the top-leve=
l company that controls OpenAI=E2=80=99s business, really is incorporated a=
s a nonprofit. It really was formed to work for the benefit of humanity and=
 not =E2=80=9Cfor the private gain of any person.=E2=80=9D And it really di=
d take donations from Musk and use them to build its team. But it eventuall=
y set up a=C2=A0for-profit subsidiary, OpenAI Global LLC, which has managed=
 to raise money from investors at an $86 billion valuation, and those inves=
tors (and OpenAI=E2=80=99s employees =E2=80=94=C2=A0though not Altman) expe=
ct=C2=A0some (capped) financial return on that investment. <a href=3D"https=
://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly9vcGVuYW=
kuY29tL291ci1zdHJ1Y3R1cmU/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC4177dd42" itemprop=3D"St=
oryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> OpenA=
I says</a> that=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cit became increasingly clear that donations a=
lone would not scale with the cost of computational power and talent requir=
ed to push core research forward, jeopardizing our mission=E2=80=9D: It had=
 to raise money from investors, by promising them returns, to achieve its m=
ission. (It raised something like $130.5 million in total donations; it has=
 raised something like <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLz=
IwMjQtMDEtMDkvbWljcm9zb2Z0LXMtb3BlbmFpLXRpZXMtZmFjZS1wb3RlbnRpYWwtZXUtbWVyZ=
2VyLWludmVzdGlnYXRpb24_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMwMTI0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1l=
bWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjQwMzAxJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1=
tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf2bcaa56" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" ite=
mscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  $13 billion</a> =
in investment commitments from Microsoft.)</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am sure OpenAI had good lawyers when it set =
up this structure, and I assume=C2=A0that as a technical matter none of thi=
s violates the certificate of incorporation or the nonprofit mission: A por=
tion of the profits of=C2=A0<em>OpenAI Global LLC </em>can go to employees =
and venture capitalists and Microsoft, even though =E2=80=9Cno part of the =
net income or assets of=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<em>OpenAI Inc.</em>=C2=A0can. Still =
that is rather technical, and Musk has a point here:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In 2017, Mr. Brockman and others s=
uggested transforming OpenAI, Inc. from a nonprofit to a for-profit corpora=
tion. After a series of communications over several weeks, Mr. Musk told Mr=
. Brockman, Dr. Sutskever, and Mr. Altman =E2=80=9C[e]ither go do something=
 on your own or continue with OpenAI as a nonprofit. I will no longer fund =
OpenAI until you have made a firm commitment to stay or I=E2=80=99m just be=
ing a fool who is essentially providing free funding to a startup. Discussi=
ons are over.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That was before OpenAI launched its for-profit=
 subsidiary, though. When it did, Musk sort of grudgingly tolerated it<a cl=
ass=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparen=
t !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On March 11, 2019, OpenAI, Inc. announced that=
 it would be creating a for-profit subsidiary: OpenAI, L.P.<a class=3D"foot=
note-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !importan=
t; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> Prospective investors were notified of an =E2=80=9Cimportant warning=
=E2=80=9D at the top of the summary term sheet that the for-profit entity =
=E2=80=9Cexists to advance OpenAI Inc.=E2=80=99s [the nonprofit=E2=80=99s] =
mission of ensuring that safe artificial general intelligence is developed =
and benefits all of humanity. The General Partner=E2=80=99s duty to this mi=
ssion and the principles advanced in the OpenAI Inc. Charter take precedenc=
e over any obligation to generate a profit.=E2=80=9D Accordingly, investors=
 were expressly advised that =E2=80=9C[i]t would be wise to view any invest=
ment in OpenAI LP in the spirit of a donation.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Following the announcement, Mr. Musk reached o=
ut to Mr. Altman asking him to =E2=80=9Cbe explicit that I have no financia=
l interest in the for-profit arm of OpenAI.=E2=80=9D However, Mr. Musk cont=
inued to support OpenAI, Inc., the non-profit, donating an additional $3.48=
 million in 2019.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But at that point he <em>was=C2=A0</em>just pr=
oviding free funding to a startup, wasn=E2=80=99t he?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So Musk argues that they had a deal, and that =
OpenAI breached it in three ways. First, it licenses GPT-4, its most powerf=
ul model so far, to Microsoft. OpenAI, in its public statements and its cha=
rter and its deal with Microsoft, has said that it will seek to=C2=A0build=
=C2=A0<em>artificial general intelligence=C2=A0</em>for the benefit of huma=
nity, but that it can license lesser forms of artificial intelligence to Mi=
crosoft. So the question is: Is GPT-4 artificial general intelligence? Musk=
 says yes:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">GPT-4 is not just capable of reasoning. It is =
better at reasoning than average humans. It scored in the 90th percentile o=
n the Uniform Bar Exam for lawyers. It scored in the 99th percentile on the=
 GRE Verbal Assessment. It even scored a 77% on the Advanced Sommelier exam=
ination. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">GPT-4 is an AGI algorithm, and hence expressly=
 outside the scope of Microsoft=E2=80=99s September 2020 exclusive license =
with OpenAI. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Under its new Board, it is not just developing=
 but is actually refining an AGI to maximize profits for Microsoft, rather =
than for the benefit of humanity.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Seems like a stretch, though Musk quotes =E2=
=80=9CMicrosoft=E2=80=99s own researchers=E2=80=9D saying that GPT-4 =E2=80=
=9Ccould reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete version of =
an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Second, GPT-4 is not open-source:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">GPT-4=E2=80=99s internal design wa=
s kept and remains a complete secret except to OpenAI=E2=80=94and, on infor=
mation and belief, Microsoft. There are no scientific publications describi=
ng the design of GPT-4. Instead, there are just press releases bragging abo=
ut performance. On information and belief, this secrecy is primarily driven=
 by commercial considerations, not safety. Although developed by OpenAI usi=
ng contributions from=C2=A0Plaintiff and others that were intended to benef=
it the public, GPT-4 is now a <em>de facto</em> Microsoft proprietary algor=
ithm, which it has integrated into its Office software suite.</p></blockquo=
te>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Again, there doesn=E2=80=99t seem to be=C2=A0a=
ny actual agreement between OpenAI and Musk (or anyone else) promising to m=
ake everything open-source, but, sure, it=E2=80=99s annoying that they buil=
t a model partly using his money and now won=E2=80=99t let him see it.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Third, Musk objects to OpenAI =E2=80=9Cpermitt=
ing Microsoft, a publicly traded for-profit corporation, to occupy a seat o=
n OpenAI, Inc.=E2=80=99s Board of Directors and exert undue influence and c=
ontrol over OpenAI=E2=80=99s non-profit activities.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Microsoft=
 is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34535030.25637=
6/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMDUvbWljc=
m9zb2Z0LXBpY2tzLWRlZS10ZW1wbGV0b24tYXMtb3BlbmFpLWJvYXJkLW9ic2VydmVyP2NtcGlk=
PUJCRDAzMDEyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV=
0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDMwMSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b852=
4a319a23cB71e3e4b0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">  just an observer</a> on the board, not a vo=
ting member, but, right, its interests in OpenAI are probably=C2=A0not part=
icularly <em>charitable</em>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And Musk is asking the court, not for his dona=
tions back, but for an order making OpenAI do what it supposedly promised t=
o do: Open up GPT-4=E2=80=99s source code, make it freely available to the =
public, end Microsoft=E2=80=99s exclusive license and board rights, and gen=
erally stop OpenAI=E2=80=99s for-profit work.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Obviously one should be pretty cynical=C2=A0he=
re.=C2=A0<em>Musk runs a for-profit artificial intelligence company</em>, x=
AI, which competes with OpenAI and has <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZX=
dzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMjAvbXVzay1zLXhhaS1zZWN1cmVzLTUwMC1taWxsaW9uLXRvd=

m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  ra=
ised money</a> by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQ=

bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  citing OpenAI=E2=80=99s com=
mercial success</a>. Blowing up that competitor=E2=80=99s commercial prospe=
cts, as this lawsuit is trying to do, could help xAI. He also runs=C2=A0oth=
er companies =E2=80=94=C2=A0Tesla Inc., Twitter/X =E2=80=94=C2=A0that make =
use of AI. I suppose Musk=E2=80=99s companies would benefit from reading Op=
enAI=E2=80=99s source code and =E2=80=9Cscientific publications describing =
the design of GPT-4,=E2=80=9D so why not sue OpenAI and try to make that in=
formation public?=C2=A0Musk=E2=80=99s protests about OpenAI=E2=80=99s unsee=
mly pursuit of AI profit for investors do look a little insincere, since he=
=E2=80=99s doing the same thing.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But he does have kind of a reasonable gripe? O=
penAI was founded as a nonprofit, raised a bunch of money from him as a don=
or to a nonprofit, and is now somehow an enormously=C2=A0valuable tech star=
tup owned by people who are not him.=C2=A0After OpenAI fired Altman last No=
vember, but before it brought him back, it looked as if OpenAI=E2=80=99s=C2=
=A0<em>for-profit investors=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0Microsoft, but also so=
me venture capitalists, and the employees who owned quasi-equity =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0had lost a bunch of value. I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbu=
siness.com/click/34535030.256376/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW=
9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTEtMjEvb3BlbmFpLWlzLWEtc3RyYW5nZS1ub25wcm9maXQ_Y21wa=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I feel like the lesson here is =E2=
=80=A6=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t invest in nonprofits at an $86 billion v=
aluation.=E2=80=9D Which I think has never come up before? Like as far as I=
 can tell no one in human history has ever purchased shares in a nonprofit =
at an $86 billion valuation? Because purchasing shares in a nonprofit, at a=
ny valuation, is not a coherent thing to do? But then OpenAI made it happen=
, for the first time, and probably also the last.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But it turns out the investors were fine,<a cl=
ass=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparen=
t !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0and the correct lesson might be the opposite one: Don=E2=80=99t=
=C2=A0<em>donate money </em>to a nonprofit that is selling shares at an $86=
 billion valuation!<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; ba=
ckground-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a> You might be skeptical about putting an $86 billion valuation on a non=
profit, but probably you should be even more skeptical that a business with=
 an $86 billion valuation is a nonprofit.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An alleged Ponzi scheme run by a Harvard MBA w=
ho solicited money from fellow alumni of the prestigious US business school=
 has been shut down by a New York court after collecting more than $2.9mn.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">New York attorney-general Letitia James said T=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThat guy was my Harvard Business Scho=

n=E2=80=99t running a Ponzi because that would be a waste of the value of h=

=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Since 2021, Artamonov has secured at least $2.=
9 million from at least 29 individual investors and engaged in a Ponzi sche=
me by paying the existing investors with the new investors=E2=80=99 funds. =
...=C2=A0Artamonov also used his investors=E2=80=99 money to fund unauthori=
zed personal expenses for vacations, shopping, and dining.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also his pitch was wild:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The OAG alleged=C2=A0that Artamono=
v lured clients by claiming that he could learn which investments Berkshire=
 Hathaway would make ahead of the market by examining public state insuranc=
e filings. Artamonov boasted to his investors that it is like =E2=80=9Chavi=
ng a private time machine=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cgetting tomorrow=E2=80=99s =
newspaper today,=E2=80=9D and projected returns of 500-1,000 percent. In re=
ality, Artamonov used his investors=E2=80=99 money to buy short-term option=
s that expired within days of purchase and appeared to have no relation to =
Berkshire Hathaway or its investment activities.=C2=A0Artamonov lost millio=
ns of investors=E2=80=99 funds by investing in these short-term options, bu=
t did not disclose the loss to his investors. To cover up the losses, Artam=
onov=C2=A0told the investors that it had been a =E2=80=9Cquiet=E2=80=9D mon=
th and to just wait and see.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CBerkshire Hathaway is required to pub=
licly disclose its investments in state insurance filings before it disclos=
es them in SEC filings, and I will read the insurance filings to make infor=
med trades=E2=80=9D is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0<em>wrong</em>, sure, but it has the =
vague shape of something that could be true. But what about =E2=80=9C=E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0and those informed trades will generate projected returns of 1,000=
%=E2=80=9D? What are they teaching kids at Harvard these days? I=C2=A0feel =
like if=C2=A0<em>I=C2=A0</em>were pitching a Ponzi scheme to the Harvard Bu=
siness School alumni, I would be promoting fake returns of, like, 35% with =
a high Sharpe ratio. You know: <em>good</em>, but not=C2=A0<em>obviously fa=
ke</em>. But apparently =E2=80=9CI have a time machine that gives me guaran=
teed 1,000% returns=E2=80=9D works on Harvard MBAs too.</p>
